content.text: The core mindset of an Essentialist is: '{{c1::Less but better}}' (discern the vital few from the trivial many).
content.explanation: Essentialism is not about how to get more things done; it's about how to get the right things done. It is a systematic discipline for cataloging and eliminating non-essentials.
content.spanish: La mentalidad central de un Esencialista es: 'Menos pero mejor' (discernir los pocos vitales de los muchos triviales).
content.usage: Instead of asking 'How can I fit all these meetings in?', ask 'Which of these meetings is truly vital?'

content.text: Essentialists view choice as an {{c1::active action}}, not a passive thing. While you may not control your options, you always control how you choose.
content.explanation: When we surrender our ability to choose, we give others permission to choose for us.
content.spanish: Los esencialistas ven la elección como una acción activa, no como algo pasivo. Aunque no controles tus opciones, controlas cómo eliges.

content.text: Instead of asking 'How can I do both?', the Essentialist asks: '{{c1::Which problem do I want to solve?}}', embracing trade-offs as an inherent part of life.
content.explanation: We cannot have it all. Making a trade-off is a strategic decision to prioritize one thing over another.
content.spanish: En lugar de preguntar '¿Cómo puedo hacer ambas cosas?', el esencialista pregunta: '¿Qué problema quiero resolver?'.

content.text: To discern what is vital, you must design space to {{c1::escape}}—creating uninterrupted time to think, focus, and reflect.
content.explanation: If you don't create space to think, the noise of the world will dictate your focus. We need space to look at the big picture.
content.spanish: Para discernir lo que es vital, debes diseñar un espacio para escapar: crear tiempo ininterrumpido para pensar.
content.usage: Block 1 hour of 'thinking time' on your calendar every day, with no meetings or slack notifications.

content.text: Essentialists protect their assets by protecting their {{c1::sleep}}, viewing it as a prerequisite for high performance rather than a waste of time.
content.explanation: One hour of sleep yields multiple hours of high-productivity and clear decision-making capacity during the day.
content.spanish: Los esencialistas protegen sus activos protegiendo su sueño, viéndolo como un prerrequisito para el alto rendimiento.

content.text: The rule of selection in Essentialism states: 'If it isn't a {{c1::clear yes}}, then it's a {{c2::clear no}}'.
content.explanation: If you are evaluating an opportunity and feel only moderately excited (e.g. 70/100), reject it. Only accept opportunities that are 90+ score.
content.spanish: La regla de selección en el Esencialismo establece: 'Si no es un sí claro, entonces es un no claro'.

content.text: Instead of broad mission statements, organizations need an {{c1::essential intent}}—a concrete, inspirational, and measurable goal.
content.explanation: An essential intent is both concrete and inspirational. It guides all micro-decisions. Example: 'Get internet access to 100 million rural households by 2028.'
content.spanish: En lugar de declaraciones de misión amplias, las organizaciones necesitan una intención esencial: una meta concreta y medible.

content.text: To protect your time, you must learn to say 'No' with {{c1::respect and courage}}, decoupling the refusal from the relationship.
content.explanation: People respect those who set clear boundaries. Saying 'No' to a request is saying 'Yes' to your priorities.
content.spanish: Para proteger tu tiempo, debes aprender a decir 'No' con respeto y coraje, separando el rechazo de la relación.
content.usage: If a manager asks for a task that isn't essential, list your active projects and ask which one should be delayed.

content.text: To stop throwing good money/time after bad, you must overcome the {{c1::sunk-cost fallacy}}—the bias that makes you continue a failing project because you've already invested in it.
content.explanation: Admit mistakes and cut losses immediately. Ask: 'If I wasn't already invested in this project, would I start it today?'
content.spanish: Para dejar de desperdiciar tiempo, debes superar la falacia del costo hundido: continuar un proyecto fallido por lo ya invertido.

content.text: A good editor adds value by {{c1::eliminating non-essentials}}, clarifying meaning, and focusing the reader's attention.
content.explanation: Editing is not just pruning; it is an active design process to maximize impact by removing distractions.
content.spanish: Un buen editor agrega valor al eliminar lo no esencial, aclarando el significado y enfocando la atención.
content.usage: Regularly audit your project list, meetings, and codebase to remove legacy clutter.

content.text: Setting clear {{c1::boundaries}} actually increases your freedom by protecting you from the agendas of others.
content.explanation: If you don't set boundaries, you will become a tool for other people's goals. Boundaries keep you focused on your essential intent.
content.spanish: Establecer límites claros en realidad aumenta tu libertad al protegerte de las agendas de los demás.

content.text: To handle the unexpected, Essentialists design a {{c1::buffer}}—adding 50% extra time or resources to project estimates.
content.explanation: The planning fallacy makes us assume things will go perfectly. A buffer protects us from stress and delays.
content.spanish: Para manejar lo inesperado, los Esencialistas diseñan un colchón (buffer): agregar un 50% de tiempo adicional.
content.usage: If a feature estimate is 10 days, report it as 15 days to build in a buffer for bugs and deployment issues.

content.text: To increase output, focus on {{c1::removing bottlenecks}} and constraints rather than trying to force more resources into the system.
content.explanation: Identify the slowest link in the process (the constraint). Removing it yields immediate, systemic progress.
content.spanish: Para aumentar el rendimiento, enfócate en eliminar los cuellos de botella en lugar de forzar más recursos.
content.usage: In coding, identify the slowest database query that blocks the API thread and optimize it first.

content.text: Essentialists focus on {{c1::small, incremental wins}} rather than big, heroic efforts, as small wins build unstoppable momentum.
content.explanation: A series of small, daily progresses creates a positive feedback loop that outperforms massive, erratic pushes.
content.spanish: Los esencialistas se enfocan en pequeñas victorias incrementales en lugar de esfuerzos grandes y heroicos.

content.text: To make execution effortless, design a {{c1::routine}} that makes the essential behaviors the default option.
content.explanation: Routines conserve mental energy by automating decisions. Once a routine is built, execution becomes automatic.
content.spanish: Para que la ejecución sea sin esfuerzo, diseña una rutina que convierta los comportamientos esenciales en la opción predeterminada.
content.usage: Design a morning routine where your first 90 minutes are dedicated entirely to coding without check email.

content.text: To be truly present, you must ask yourself: '{{c1::What is important right now?}}' (WIN).
content.explanation: Do not let your mind get trapped in past mistakes or future worries. Focus on the single action you can take in the present moment.
content.spanish: Para estar verdaderamente presente, debes preguntarte: '¿Qué es importante en este momento?' (WIN).

content.text: The Non-Essentialist believes: '{{c1::I have to do it all}}', leading to being stretched thin in a mile-wide, inch-deep layout.
content.explanation: Non-essentialists say yes to everything, get overwhelmed, and make only millimeter progress in a thousand directions.
content.spanish: El No Esencialista cree: 'Tengo que hacerlo todo', lo que lleva a estar disperso en muchas direcciones sin profundidad.

content.text: Essentialists view {{c1::play}} as an essential catalyst for creativity, observation, and stress reduction, not as a waste of time.
content.explanation: Play opens the mind's diffuse mode, helping us see connections and solutions we missed during focused stress.
content.spanish: Los esencialistas ven el juego (play) como un catalizador esencial para la creatividad y la reducción del estrés.

content.text: The book compares organizing your life to {{c1::cleaning a closet}}: you must actively select what to keep, discard the rest, and audit regularly.
content.explanation: If you don't actively clean your closet, it fills up with clothes you don't wear. Life fills up with non-essential commitments similarly.
content.spanish: El libro compara organizar tu vida con limpiar un armario: debes seleccionar activamente qué conservar y descartar el resto.

content.text: Instead of FOMO (Fear of Missing Out), Essentialists embrace {{c1::JOMO}} (Joy of Missing Out)—celebrating the choice to reject the trivial.
content.explanation: Knowing that you rejected a distraction to focus on a vital priority brings peace and satisfaction.
content.spanish: En lugar de FOMO, los esencialistas adoptan JOMO (el placer de perderse las cosas), celebrando la elección de rechazar lo trivial.

content.text: The core premise of Oliver Burkeman is that the average human lifespan is around {{c1::four thousand weeks}}, making time management a matter of radical prioritization.
content.explanation: Accepting your finitude is the key to psychological peace. You will never have time for everything, so choose what to ignore.
content.spanish: La premisa central de Oliver Burkeman es que la vida humana promedio es de unas cuatro mil semanas, lo que hace crucial la priorización.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: The Efficiency Trap ⏳
content.text: The {{c1::Efficiency Trap}} states that becoming more efficient at clearing tasks only generates more tasks to fill the space.
content.explanation: If you reply to emails faster, you get more replies back. If you clear tasks faster, you get assigned more work. You can't beat the queue.
content.spanish: La trampa de la eficiencia establece que volverse más eficiente al completar tareas solo genera más tareas para llenar el espacio.

content.text: Convenience culture makes things frictionless, but it strips away the {{c1::meaning and connection}} of experiences that require time and effort.
content.explanation: Frictionless tasks feel easy, but they isolate us and make us lose patience with things that cannot be accelerated (relationships, deep learning).
content.spanish: La cultura de la conveniencia elimina la fricción, pero también elimina el significado y la conexión de las experiencias.

content.text: Instead of trying to avoid procrastination, practice {{c1::active procrastination}}—consciously choosing what to neglect so you can focus on the vital.
content.explanation: You will always procrastinate on something. The question is whether you procrastinate on the trivial or the vital.
content.spanish: En lugar de intentar evitar la procrastinación, practica la procrastinación activa: elegir conscientemente qué descuidar.

content.text: Peace comes from living a {{c1::limit-embracing}} life—accepting that you cannot make every choice, read every book, or visit every country.
content.explanation: The anxiety of choice disappears when you accept that choosing one thing means rejecting all other options by definition.
content.spanish: La paz proviene de vivir una vida que acepta los límites, admitiendo que no puedes elegirlo todo.

content.text: Social media sites are designed to monetize your {{c1::attention}}, distracting you from the finite weeks of your actual life.
content.explanation: Your attention is your life. Distraction is a form of robbery of the finite weeks you have on earth.
content.spanish: Las redes sociales están diseñadas para monetizar tu atención, distrayéndote de las semanas finitas de tu vida real.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: The Joy of Doing Nothing ⏳
content.text: To reclaim your time, practice the art of {{c1::doing nothing}}—resting without the pressure of being productive or optimized.
content.explanation: We treat rest as a way to recover for more work (utility rest). True leisure has value in itself, not as utility.
content.spanish: Para recuperar tu tiempo, practica el arte de no hacer nada: descansar sin la presión de ser productivo.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: The Next-Step Fallacy ⏳
content.text: The tendency to view the present moment only as a vehicle to reach some future state or milestone is the {{c1::next-step fallacy}}.
content.explanation: If you only live for the next milestone (promotion, vacation, retirement), you lose the ability to experience your life as it actually happens.
content.spanish: La tendencia a ver el momento presente solo como un vehículo para llegar a un estado futuro es la falacia del paso siguiente.

content.text: Keep a {{c1::fixed volume}} limit on your active projects (e.g. maximum of 3), placing all others on a backlog queue until one is finished.
content.explanation: Multitasking splits focus and delays all projects. Finishing one project before starting the next speeds up delivery.
content.spanish: Mantén un límite de volumen fijo en tus proyectos activos (máximo 3), colocando los demás en cola de espera.
content.usage: Limit your Kanban board active column to 3 cards maximum.

content.text: The core paradox of productivity is: if you get faster at work, you get more work, because the volume of demands on your time is {{c1::infinite}}.
content.explanation: Demands are infinite, but time is finite. Trying to solve the equation by moving faster is mathematically impossible.
content.spanish: La paradoja central de la productividad: si trabajas más rápido, obtienes más trabajo, porque las demandas son infinitas.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: The Decisive No ⏳
content.text: Every commitment requires a {{c1::decisive sacrifice}} of other possibilities. True commitment means closing off options.
content.explanation: If you keep options open, you commit to nothing deeply. Committing to a project or relationship requires letting go of alternatives.
content.spanish: Cada compromiso requiere un sacrificio decisivo de otras posibilidades. Comprometerse de verdad exige cerrar opciones.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: Leisure as an End ⏳
content.text: True leisure is an {{c1::autotelic}} activity—something done for its own sake, not to achieve a goal or improve yourself.
content.explanation: Playing a game, drawing, or walking just for the joy of it, not to post on social media or get fit.
content.spanish: El verdadero ocio es una actividad autotélica: algo que se hace por el placer de hacerlo, no para lograr una meta.

content.text: Technology has made things so fast that we get caught in an {{c1::impatience spiral}}, getting angry when a web page takes 2 seconds to load.
content.explanation: The faster things become, the more painful the remaining friction feels.
content.spanish: La tecnología ha hecho que todo sea tan rápido que caemos en una espiral de impaciencia cuando algo tarda unos segundos.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: Staying on the Bus ⏳
content.text: To achieve mastery or depth in a career or project, you must practice {{c1::staying on the bus}}—resisting the urge to switch paths when things get boring.
content.explanation: Mastery requires pushing past the initial excitement into the plateau of deep practice.
content.spanish: Para lograr el dominio en un proyecto, debes practicar 'quedarte en el autobús', resistiendo la tentación de cambiar de rumbo.

content.text: Embrace your {{c1::cosmic insignificance}}—accepting that your life does not need to change the course of human history to be meaningful.
content.explanation: Lowers the pressure to be 'extraordinary' and allows you to enjoy simple, daily acts of kindness and connection.
content.spanish: Acepta tu insignificancia cósmica, admitiendo que tu vida no necesita cambiar la historia para tener sentido.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: The Limit of Control ⏳
content.text: You do not own your time; you can only {{c1::participate in it}} moment by moment. Trying to control the future causes anxiety.
content.explanation: We plan to secure the future, but the future is always uncertain. Live the present.
content.spanish: No eres dueño de tu tiempo; solo puedes participar en él momento a momento.

content.text: Burkeman recommends keeping a {{c1::closed list}} of tasks—items you commit to doing today, leaving all others on an open list.
content.explanation: An open list is a source of anxiety. A closed list is a boundary that defines success for today.
content.spanish: Burkeman recomienda mantener una lista cerrada de tareas: elementos que te comprometes a realizar hoy.
content.usage: Move 5 tasks from your general backlog to your 'Today' list and do not add others.

content.scenario: Four Thousand Weeks: The Slow-Down Strategy ⏳
content.text: When faced with an overwhelming volume of work, the best response is to {{c1::slow down}} and do one task with complete attention.
content.explanation: Rushing causes errors and anxiety. Slowing down restores focus and quality.
content.spanish: Ante un volumen abrumador de trabajo, la mejor respuesta es reducir la velocidad y hacer una tarea con total atención.

content.text: Time is valuable only when it is shared. Having complete freedom over your schedule is useless if you have no {{c1::shared time}} with friends and family.
content.explanation: Solitary time lacks the coordinate rhythm of shared community events.
content.spanish: El tiempo solo es valioso cuando se comparte. Tener control total de tu agenda es inútil sin tiempo compartido.

content.text: Patience is the willingness to let things take the time they {{c1::actually require}} rather than trying to force them to fit your schedule.
content.explanation: Allows you to engage with difficult tasks like coding complex architectures, reading dense books, or building relationships.
content.spanish: La paciencia es la disposición a permitir que las cosas tomen el tiempo que realmente requieren.

content.text: The Fogg Behavior Model states that behavior occurs when three elements converge at the same moment: **B = MAP**, which stands for {{c1::Motivation}}, {{c2::Ability}}, and a {{c3::Prompt}}.
content.explanation: If any of these three elements is missing, the behavior will not occur. When a behavior fails, check which element was lacking.
content.spanish: El Modelo de Comportamiento de Fogg establece que el comportamiento ocurre cuando convergen tres elementos: Motivación, Habilidad y un Recordatorio (Prompt).

content.text: In the Fogg Behavior Model, the most important element for initiating action immediately is the {{c1::Prompt}} (recordatorio/desencadenante).
content.explanation: No behavior happens without a prompt. You can have high motivation and ability, but without a prompt, the action remains inactive.
content.spanish: En el Modelo de Comportamiento de Fogg, el elemento más importante para iniciar la acción es el Prompt (recordatorio/desencadenante).

content.text: The Fogg Behavior Grid shows that a behavior will occur if it falls above the {{c1::Action Line}}—meaning the combination of motivation and ability is high enough when the prompt fires.
content.explanation: If a task requires high ability (it is very hard) and motivation is low, the prompt will fail because it lies below the Action Line.
content.spanish: La cuadrícula de Fogg muestra que un comportamiento ocurrirá si cae por encima de la Línea de Acción.

content.text: BJ Fogg argues that the easiest way to make a behavior more likely to occur is to focus on increasing {{c1::Ability}} by making the behavior simpler.
content.explanation: Motivation is volatile and unreliable. Simplicity is reliable. If you make the behavior tiny (e.g. floss one tooth), it requires almost zero motivation.
content.spanish: BJ Fogg sostiene que la forma más fácil de hacer que ocurra un comportamiento es aumentar la Habilidad (Ability) haciéndolo más simple.

content.text: An {{c1::Anchor Prompt}} is an existing, solid routine in your daily life (like brushing your teeth or pouring coffee) that you use to trigger your new tiny habit.
content.explanation: Instead of relying on digital alarms, anchor your new habit to a behavior you already perform automatically.
content.spanish: Un Recordatorio Ancla (Anchor Prompt) es una rutina sólida existente en tu vida que utilizas para activar tu nuevo hábito.
content.usage: Anchor recipe: 'After I pour my morning coffee (Anchor), I will write down my 3 daily goals (New Tiny Habit).'

content.text: A tiny habit recipe follows the formula: 'After I [Anchor], I will [{{c1::Tiny Behavior}}]'.
content.explanation: Keep the behavior tiny. It should take less than 30 seconds and require minimal effort.
content.spanish: Una receta de hábitos diminutos sigue la fórmula: 'Después de [Ancla], haré [Comportamiento Diminuto]'.
content.usage: Example: 'After I close my bedroom door, I will do 2 push-ups.'

content.text: To wire a new habit into your brain, you must perform an immediate {{c1::celebration}} right after completing the tiny behavior, which releases dopamine.
content.explanation: Habits are formed by emotions, not by repetition. Feeling successful immediately after the action signals the brain to encode the routine.
content.spanish: Para fijar un nuevo hábito en tu cerebro, debes realizar una celebración inmediata justo después de completar el comportamiento.
content.usage: Right after doing 2 pushups, give a fist pump and say 'Awesome!' or smile broadly.

content.text: BJ Fogg identifies 5 factors that affect Ability (the Simplicity Factors): {{c1::Time}}, {{c2::Money}}, {{c3::Physical Effort}}, {{c4::Brain Cycles}}, and {{c5::Social Deviance}}.
content.explanation: If a behavior requires too much of any of these factors, it is hard and less likely to happen.
content.spanish: BJ Fogg identifica 5 factores de simplicidad que afectan la Habilidad: Tiempo, Dinero, Esfuerzo Físico, Ciclos Mentales y Desviación Social.

content.text: A {{c1::Golden Behavior}} is an action that is highly effective at achieving your goal, you have the ability to do, and you are naturally motivated to do.
content.explanation: Do not force yourself to do behaviors you hate. Find the intersection of impact, ability, and motivation.
content.spanish: Un Comportamiento Dorado (Golden Behavior) es una acción efectiva, que tienes la capacidad de hacer y que quieres hacer.

content.text: To grow a habit, start by planting a seed—a tiny version of the habit. As the routine becomes automatic, the habit will {{c1::naturally expand}} to its full size.
content.explanation: Once flossing one tooth is automatic, you will naturally start flossing all of them because the friction of starting is gone.
content.spanish: Para cultivar un hábito, comienza plantando una semilla (una versión diminuta). El hábito se expandirá naturalmente.

content.text: A {{c1::Pearl Habit}} is a routine where you take an annoying, unavoidable cue (like a crying baby, a noisy neighbor, or traffic) and use it as a prompt for a positive behavior.
content.explanation: Named after pearls, which are formed around irritating grains of sand. Transforms friction into growth.
content.spanish: Un Hábito Perla (Pearl Habit) es una rutina en la que tomas una señal molesta e inevitable y la usas como recordatorio positivo.
content.usage: Cue: Siren outside. Routine: Take one deep breath and relax shoulders.

content.text: The {{c1::Motivation Wave}} refers to temporary surges of high motivation that allow you to do hard, one-time behaviors (like buying gym equipment or scheduling a doctor's checkup).
content.explanation: Do not build daily habits that rely on the peak of the wave. Use the wave only to build systems and make future habits easier.
content.spanish: La Ola de Motivación se refiere a oleadas temporales de alta motivación que te permiten realizar conductas difíciles de una sola vez.

content.text: To stop a bad habit, focus on {{c1::removing the prompt}} rather than trying to resist the behavior with willpower.
content.explanation: If you don't receive the trigger, the routine never starts, preserving your willpower.
content.spanish: Para detener un mal hábito, enfócate en eliminar el recordatorio (prompt) en lugar de resistirte con fuerza de voluntad.
content.usage: If you check your phone too much at work, put the phone in another room (removing the prompt).

content.text: For a celebration to trigger dopamine release and form a habit, it must occur within {{c1::two seconds}} of the behavior.
content.explanation: Delaying the celebration breaks the associative learning loop in the brain.
content.spanish: Para que una celebración active la dopamina, debe ocurrir dentro de los dos segundos posteriores al comportamiento.

content.text: Bundling a new tiny habit with an anchor and an immediate celebration is called creating a {{c1::habit recipe}}.
content.explanation: Simple formula for designing behavioral change.
content.spanish: Agrupar un nuevo hábito diminuto con un ancla y una celebración inmediata se llama crear una receta de hábitos.

content.text: The ultimate goal of Tiny Habits is not just behavior change, but an {{c1::identity shift}}—viewing yourself as the type of person who easily executes these behaviors.
content.explanation: Small wins build self-efficacy, which alters your self-image. Once you believe 'I am a healthy person', healthy choices become effortless.
content.spanish: El objetivo final de Tiny Habits es un cambio de identidad: verte a ti mismo como el tipo de persona que realiza estas acciones.

content.text: BJ Fogg identifies 3 types of prompts: **Person prompts** (internal urges), **Context prompts** (alarms/notes), and **Action prompts** (existing routines). {{c1::Action prompts}} are the most effective for habit building.
content.explanation: Person prompts fail because memory is unreliable. Context prompts fail because they cause alarm fatigue. Action prompts capitalize on existing brain wiring.
content.spanish: Fogg identifica 3 tipos de recordatorios: Personales, de Contexto y de Acción. Los de Acción son los más efectivos.

content.scenario: Tiny Habits: The Swarm of Behaviors 📈
content.text: To solve a broad goal (like 'reduce stress'), generate a {{c1::swarm of behaviors}} (a list of 20-30 specific, actionable habits) and select the easiest golden behaviors.
content.explanation: Broad goals are not actionable. Breaking them down into a swarm of micro-actions lets you pick the path of least resistance.
content.spanish: Para resolver un objetivo amplio, genera un enjambre de comportamientos (una lista de 20-30 hábitos específicos) y elige el más fácil.

content.text: If you forget to do a tiny habit, do not criticize yourself. Instead, adjust the recipe, make it {{c1::even smaller}}, or find a better anchor.
content.explanation: Self-criticism activates the threat brain, reducing motivation. Iteration and optimization are the keys to habit success.
content.spanish: Si olvidas realizar un hábito diminuto, no te critiques. Ajusta la receta o hazla aún más pequeña.

content.text: BJ Fogg uses the word {{c1::Shine}} to describe the positive emotional feeling of success and self-efficacy that forms habits.
content.explanation: The feeling of 'Shine' acts as a chemical stamp, telling the brain to remember the preceding routine.
content.spanish: BJ Fogg usa la palabra 'Shine' (brillo) para describir la sensación emocional positiva de éxito que forma los hábitos.
